summary: Acoustic gems with Black Peter and Deep Elem Blues amid poor audio
blurb: The concert opens with a brief acoustic suite—Deep Elem Blues, Friend Of The Devil, Silver Threads And Golden Needles, and Black Peter. Jerry delivers a rare folk‑style vocal and strong guitar work, especially on Black Peter, but the tape is riddled with hiss and low fidelity.
review: May 9, 1970 at Worcester Polytechnic Institute offered Deadheads a fleeting taste of pure acoustic intimacy before the band moved into electric territory that this recording barely captures. The set begins with an uncharacteristically short folk‑leaning run—Deep Elem Blues rolls out with plaintive storytelling, Friend Of The Devil glides in, Silver Threads And Golden Needles adds a country flavor, and Black Peter closes the acoustic portion with haunting lyricism. Jerry’s voice is stripped down to its roots, and his acoustic guitar work feels both tender and commanding. While Pigpen’s organ peeks through on Black Peter, the rest of the electric set dissolves into a murky hiss that makes it hard to follow. For listeners who cherish those early‑70 acoustic moments, this tape holds value despite its technical shortcomings.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The sole recording analysis describes mixed sentiment: positive remarks for the acoustic set (standout Black Peter, Deep Elem Blues) balanced by criticism of hissy, low‑fidelity capture of the electric portion. This yields a modest +0.1 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Only available source; despite poor fidelity it preserves the rare acoustic set and standout songs.

key_highlights: Rare pure‑folk acoustic set early in the show
Standout performances of Black Peter and Deep Elem Blues
Recording suffers from hiss, mud, and limited capture of the electric portion

band_performance.Jerry: delivers a rare pure‑folk style vocal and strong acoustic guitar work during the opening set.
band_performance.Bob: participates in the opening acoustic numbers but receives no specific commentary on his playing.
